content.method: Natural experiment / difference-in-differences design comparing regions with varying pre-existing density of church/private alternatives, tracking family enrollment choices before and after any pilot phase-out of early entry points; supplement with survey-based discrete choice modeling of parental preferences under hypothetical structural scenarios.
content.question: If Hungary delays selection to age 14 (eliminating the 10 and 12 entry points), would displaced parental demand simply relocate to remaining selective mechanisms (church/private schools, inter-municipal transfers, within-school class differentiation) rather than reducing overall segregation?
content.why_it_matters: This is the central risk identified across the Finnish, Polish, and Portuguese lenses for the flagship reform proposal (TP-live-t2): both Finland's post-reform resurgence of within-school selection and Hungary's own church/private exemption structure suggest mechanism displacement rather than elimination is plausible. Resolving this shapes whether structural reform alone is sufficient or must be bundled with choice/admissions constraints.
